skill.name: imagegenskill
skill.description: Generate renderable, scientific-style SVG graphics directly from natural-language requirements (no image models). Use when users ask for an image/picture/scientific diagram/visualization poster or explicitly request SVG output for web-embeddable vector graphics.
skill.summary: Generate scientific-style SVG diagrams from natural language descriptions without image models.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 281 static findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The skill generates SVG graphics locally using Python math libraries and writes output files to a project-local directory. The high static risk score was driven by pattern matches in markdown reference documentation (shell command examples, SHA256 keyword occurrences, and API key references for a separate tool). The actual skill code in scripts/svg_gen.py is self-contained, has no external network calls, no credential access, and no command execution. The only confirmed risk factor is filesystem access for writing SVG output files.

low_findings.title: Local filesystem write operations in SVG generator
low_findings.description: scripts/svg_gen.py writes SVG files and JSON metadata to the output/svggen/ directory. This is the intended behavior of the skill and poses no security risk as writes are confined to a project-local directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code confirms write operations to output/svggen/ directory only. Path is hardcoded and not user-controlled.

content.user_title: Generate scientific SVG diagrams from natural language
content.value_statement: Creates renderable scientific-style SVG graphics from natural language descriptions without using AI image models. The skill transforms text prompts into structured vector graphics with grids, network layers, waveforms, and symbol rings for research posters and documentation.
actual_capabilities: Generate scientific-style SVG graphics from natural language descriptions
Apply different visual styles including lab-atlas, signal-loom, and lattice-field
Produce structured SVG output with grid, network, waveform, and symbol ring layers
Create deterministic outputs using seed-based generation for reproducibility
Write SVG files and JSON metadata to a local output directory
limitations: Output is limited to SVG vector graphics, not raster images or photographs
Visual style is constrained to scientific and technical aesthetics
Requires Python 3.8 or higher to run the generation script
Does not use AI image models; results are algorithmic and not photorealistic

use_cases.title: Research poster creation for scientists
use_cases.description: Quickly generate scientific-style poster visuals for research presentations, conference materials, and lab documentation from a short text brief.

use_cases.title: Technical diagram generation for documentation
use_cases.description: Create technical diagrams and structured visualizations for documentation, educational slides, and technical reports without using image editing software.

use_cases.title: Concept visualization for data science
use_cases.description: Generate visual representations of abstract concepts such as latent space, network topology, and spectral analysis for data science presentations and publications.

prompt_templates.title: Basic SVG diagram generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scientific poster-style SVG showing graph topology in latent space. Use the lab-atlas style with restrained colors and high text readability. Include grid, network, and waveform layers.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a first SVG diagram from a simple text description

prompt_templates.title: Using a different visual style
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a scientific SVG visualization of spectral waveform analysis. Use the signal-loom style for denser spectral waveforms and stronger texture. Include visible frequency bands and symbol rings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Switching between available visual styles for different presentation needs

prompt_templates.title: Customizing density parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a lattice-field style SVG scientific diagram with reduced node count for a cleaner look. The visualization should show crystal lattice structures with visible grid and sparse node placement.
prompt_templates.scenario: Tuning visual density parameters for clearer, less cluttered outputs

prompt_templates.title: Reproducible generation with seed control
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a scientific SVG poster about multi-scale network topology. Use the lab-atlas style. Set specific seed and density parameters so the output remains reproducible for publication.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a deterministic output that can be exactly reproduced

output_examples.input: Scientific poster-style SVG: Graph topology in latent space with lab-atlas aesthetic
output_examples.output: A 1400x900 SVG diagram with a calm scientific aesthetic. A faint grid covers the background. Four horizontal spectral bands in muted colors arc across the canvas. Scattered network nodes with connecting lines form a cluster in the center. Triangular symbol rings surround a focal point on the left. A metadata panel in the bottom-right corner displays the mode, seed, hash, and prompt text. The overall style resembles a laboratory poster with restrained, readable colors.

output_examples.input: Signal-loom style visualization of spectral waveform analysis with dense bands
output_examples.output: A 1400x900 SVG with a darker, more textured scientific aesthetic. Seven waveform bands with stronger amplitude sweep horizontally across the canvas. A wider grid step creates more negative space. Network nodes are concentrated in a tighter central cluster. The metadata panel shows signal-loom mode. Triangular glyphs form concentric rings from the prompt text characters. The style emphasizes spectral texture and denser visual layering.

output_examples.input: Crystal lattice structure diagram with lattice-field style
output_examples.output: A 1400x900 SVG with a tighter 32px grid creating a dense lattice appearance. Three subtle waveform bands provide background texture. A higher node count (110) forms a well-connected network with many short-range edges. Lower noise point density keeps the visual clean. The metadata panel identifies lattice-field mode. The overall effect resembles a crystallographic diagram or materials science visualization.

best_practices: Write clear, descriptive prompts that specify the scientific concept, desired visual elements, and any structural requirements
Choose the appropriate style preset (lab-atlas for calm presentations, signal-loom for spectral data, lattice-field for structural diagrams)
Adjust density parameters in the script configuration when output is too cluttered or too sparse for your use case
anti_patterns: Requesting photorealistic images or raster graphics instead of vector SVG output
Using overly long prompts with irrelevant details that do not map to SVG structural elements
Expecting the skill to access the internet, use API keys, or call external image generation services

faq.question: What types of images can this skill generate?
faq.answer: This skill generates SVG vector graphics with a scientific poster aesthetic. Outputs include grid layouts, network node diagrams, waveform bands, symbol rings, and noise textures. It does not generate photographs or photorealistic images.

faq.question: Does this skill need internet access or API keys?
faq.answer: No. The SVG generator runs entirely locally using Python standard library modules. It does not call any external APIs or require any API keys.

faq.question: How do I change the visual style of the output?
faq.answer: The STYLE variable at the top of scripts/svg_gen.py supports three presets: lab-atlas (default, calm and clean), signal-loom (denser spectral waveforms), and lattice-field (prominent grid structures). Edit the STYLE value and regenerate.

faq.question: Can I reproduce the same SVG output?
faq.answer: Yes. The generator uses a deterministic seed derived from the prompt text via SHA256 hashing. The same prompt will always produce the same output. The seed value is included in the output metadata JSON file.

faq.question: Where are the generated SVG files saved?
faq.answer: SVG files and JSON metadata are saved to the output/svggen/ directory relative to the working directory. A convenience file named latest.svg is updated on each run for quick preview.

faq.question: What Python version is required?
faq.answer: Python 3.8 or higher is required. The script uses only Python standard library modules including hashlib, json, math, os, random, and time. No third-party packages are needed.
